items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.

items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: Whose 1967 Treatise is a famous graphic score?
items.why: Correct answer: Cornelius Cardew. “John Cage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was MIDI standardised?
items.why: It's 1983. “1979” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Around what year did Les Paul pioneer multitrack recording?
items.why: Exact answer: 1947 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
